exemplar.title: To investigate how information supplied after an event influences an eyewitness' memory of that event

detailedExemplarData.title: To investigate how information supplied after an event influences an eyewitness' memory of that event

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: The investigation aim is clearly stated and its relevance to criticalâthinking and academic contexts is well explained.
Reconstructive memory and schema theory are described accurately and linked coherently to the studyâs manipulations.
Independent and dependent variables are operationalized precisely, making the hypothesis testable and replicable.
Both null and directional hypotheses are articulated correctly, referencing operationalized conditions and anticipated significant differences.
weaknesses: The theoretical description, while accurate, omits some nuances (e.g., sourceâmonitoring processes) that would deepen the background.
The summary of the Loftus & Palmer study is incomplete, missing details of participant allocation and measures of variability.
The rationale paragraph is somewhat brief and could benefit from concrete classroom examples to strengthen realâworld applicability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student meets most descriptors in the top band, demonstrating a clear aim, full operationalization of variables, and strong linkage to theory. Minor omissions in theoretical depth and study summary prevent a full mark, so a solid 5/6 is justified.

strengths: The independentâmeasures design is not only described but also explained, showing understanding of how order effects are mitigated.
Sampling techniques are both described and justified, with a clear rationale for age and educational background control.
Materials and control variables are listed comprehensively and tied to theoretical considerations, ensuring replicability.
weaknesses: Discussion of control variables omits factors such as screen size and ambient noise control.
Procedural timings (e.g., video duration, questionnaire completion time) are not specified.
Selfâselection bias in voluntary sampling is not addressed or mitigated explicitly.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Design and sampling strands clearly meet the top band (4/4), and materials are sufficiently explained, though with some procedural gaps. The overall work warrants the maximum 4/4 for Exploration.

strengths: Appropriate choice and accurate application of descriptive and inferential statistics (MannâWhitney U) with correct decision rules.
Graphical presentation (bar graph) is correctly formatted with clear labels and directly addresses the hypothesis.
Findings are interpreted with respect to the data and linked back to schema theory, explaining the observed differences between conditions.
weaknesses: Effect size is not reported, limiting the completeness of the statistical analysis.
The data summary table and graph omit units (mph) and variability indicators (error bars).
Assumptions of the nonparametric test are not explicitly stated to justify the choice of MannâWhitney U.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates solid application of both descriptive and inferential statistics and links findings appropriately. Missing effectâsize reporting and minor presentational issues justify a high but not perfect mark of 5/6.

strengths: Findings are discussed with reference to reconstructive memory theory, reinforcing the theoretical framework.
Strengths and limitations are stated and explained with relevance to validity and hypothesis testing.
Proposed modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations and justified in terms of mitigating specific issues.
weaknesses: Discussion lacks depth in alternative explanations (e.g., demand characteristics) and boundary conditions.
The conclusion restates statistical outcomes but does not integrate limitations or modifications into a cohesive summary.
Discussion of limitations could be more rigorous by quantifying impacts (e.g., effect sizes) and addressing sampling biases such as gender representation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation meets the bandâ3â4 descriptors with coherent discussion and justified modifications. However, limited depth in broader theoretical debate and some missing evaluative elements support a conservative 4/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph of median speeds is provided with labelled axes and an appropriate title, directly addressing the hypothesis.  However, the choice of bar graph (with a single statistic) gives limited information compared to a box-and-whisker or scatterplot, and axis scaling is not shown in the excerpt.  Minor presentational weaknesses place the work in the lower half of the 5-6 descriptor, so 4 is awarded.

The student states the statistical outcome (U = 18 < 27, p < .05), explicitly rejects the null, and links the higher estimates in the âsmashedâ condition to reconstructive memory and schema activation, thereby connecting the findings to both data and hypothesis.  Interpretation is accurate but relatively brief, justifying a secure score of 5 within band 5-6.

Both IV (verb intensity: âsmashedâ vs âcontactedâ) and DV (estimated speed in mph) are clearly identified and fully operationalised.  Directional research and null hypotheses are properly written and reference âsignificant differenceâ between operationalised conditions.  This fulfils all requirements at the top of band 5-6 and is executed without error, justifying the maximum band mark.

Voluntary sampling followed by purposive selection is described and then **justified** (ease of recruitment, homogeneity of age, cognitive development).  The reasoning shows why the chosen age/IB background controls extraneous variables, fulfilling the âexplainedâ criterion for band 3-4; clarity and relevance warrant the top mark of that band.

Reconstructive memory and schema theory are described with accurate detail (memory as active reconstruction, schemas filling gaps).  The Loftus & Palmer study is summarised and used to illustrate how verb intensity activates different schemas.  The student then explicitly states how their own investigation âlinks to the theoryâ by manipulating verb intensity.  The explanation is coherent, but depth is moderate (some theoretical nuances such as source monitoring are not explored), warranting a solid but not top score within the 5-6 band.

The evaluation opens with a paragraph restating how results support reconstructive memory theory and verb-intensity effects.  While links to theory are present, the depth of discussion (e.g., alternative explanations, boundary conditions) remains limited.  This meets band 3-4, with coherent but not comprehensive discussion, earning 4.

The aim (to investigate whether the verbs âsmashedâ versus âcontactedâ influence eyewitness speed estimates) is stated explicitly and is followed by a clear paragraph explaining why this issue is important for high-school studentsâ critical-thinking skills and academic work.  The explanation explicitly links the psycholinguistic issue to real-life academic contexts, demonstrating relevance rather than merely identifying it.  Overall the student meets the descriptor for band 5-6, though the rationale remains somewhat brief and context-specific, so a mid-band score of 5 is most appropriate.

Multiple strengths and limitations are **stated and explained** (e.g., design removes order effects but may group better speed-guessers; voluntary sampling ease vs. self-selection bias).  Each point is linked to potential impact on validity, showing relevance.  Depth is adequate but not exhaustive (e.g., no discussion of demand characteristics), so a high band-3-4 score of 4 is appropriate.

Several control variables (time of day, same room, identical video) are listed and briefly rationalised; materials are enumerated with some justification (10-s clip chosen for emotional intensity comparison).  While reasons are present, the discussion is thinner than for sampling/design and lacks consideration of *all* potential confounds (e.g., screen size, ambient noise).  This meets band 3-4 but more superficially, so a score of 3 is appropriate.

For each limitation the student proposes a concrete modification (matched-pairs for individual differences, broader age range & random sampling, exam-style seating to prevent copying) and justifies how it would mitigate the issue.  The links are explicit, satisfying the descriptor for band 5-6, but justification is concise and lacks feasibility considerations, so a conservative 4 is awarded.

The independent-measures design is not only described but also **explained**âthe student clarifies why different participants remove order effects and links this to causal inference about the verb manipulation.  This explanatory component meets the descriptor for band 3-4; the account is accurate and concise, meriting the top of that band.

Both descriptive (medians, IQR, outlier test) and inferential (Mann-Whitney U with rationale for non-parametric choice, correct decision rule, p-value) statistics are applied.  No substantive computational errors are evident from the values reported.  Although effect size is absent, the overall application is accurate, satisfying band 5-6.  Minor omissions keep the score at 5 rather than 6.

annotations.comment: The student provides a clear, coherent description of reconstructive memory theory, explaining how memory actively reconstructs events by assembling fragments and schemas. This demonstrates solid theoretical grounding.
The original studyâs summary omits measures of variability (e.g., SD or SE) when reporting mean speed estimates. Including these would strengthen the description of Loftus & Palmerâs findings.

The discussion of schema theory effectively links mental frameworks to memory distortions, illustrating how preexisting beliefs fill gaps in recollection. This adds depth to the theoretical foundation.
The summary of the Loftus & Palmer (1974) study is cut mid-sentence, leaving out key methodological details (e.g., sample division and conditions). A complete account is needed for clarity.

The linkage between schema activation and the study aim is articulated clearlyâparticipantsâ speed estimates are directly tied to verb intensity and schema-driven reconstruction. This makes the aim highly coherent.
The rationale paragraph would benefit from concrete classroom examples of memory errors to reinforce relevance for high-school contexts and make the applicability more tangible.
The independent variable (verb intensity) and dependent variable (speed estimate) are operationalised with precision, making the investigation replicable and hypothesis testing straightforward.
Both directional and null hypotheses are clearly stated, referencing operationalised conditions and anticipated significant differences. This satisfies top-band requirements for hypothesis clarity.
The independent measures design is well explained, with random assignment eliminating order effects, reinforcing causal inference between verb manipulation and speed estimates.
The description of voluntary sampling does not address self-selection bias or how willingness to participate was monitored. Consider stating how this bias was mitigated.
The purposive sampling rationale is strong, explaining how age and educational background homogenise cognitive development and control extraneous variation.
The control-variable section omits critical factors like screen size, resolution, and ambient noise control. These should be specified to ensure all confounds are addressed.
The materials list in Appendix references and questionnaires is comprehensive, ensuring replicability and clear linkage to each study component.
The rationale for choosing a 10-second clip and contrasting verb intensities directly ties materials to schema theory, reinforcing theoreticalâempirical alignment.
The procedural steps do not specify the duration of video display or the time allowed for questionnaire completion. Including exact timings would improve procedural clarity.
The outlier analysis is thorough, correctly calculating quartiles and boundaries. This demonstrates strong understanding of descriptive statistics principles.
The table of medians and IQR lacks explicit axis labels and units (mph). Adding these would improve interpretability of the data summary.
When interpreting preliminary differences, explicitly stating that the âsmashedâ median (55) exceeds âcontactedâ (33.5) would strengthen the narrative cohesion.
The bar graph is correctly formatted with clear labels and directly addresses the hypothesis, facilitating visual comparison of conditions.
The justification for using the MannâWhitney U test is well articulated, considering sample size and distribution assumptions.
The inferential section would benefit from explicitly stating test assumptions (e.g., independent observations, ordinal data) to fully justify the choice of nonparametric analysis.
The bar graph omits error bars or variability indicators, which would help readers assess the distribution and reliability of the speed estimates.
The interpretation of U = 18 < 27 and rejecting the null is accurate, demonstrating correct application of the decision rule.
The evaluation discussion effectively links findings back to reconstructive memory theory, reinforcing how verb intensity shapes memory reconstruction.
The limitation regarding individual differences is noted, but quantifying between-group variance (e.g., effect size) would contextualise the impact more rigorously.
The matched-pairs design modification is well-justified, directly addressing the individual differences limitation through pre-testing and pairing.
To improve representativeness and reduce volunteer bias, consider stratified sampling across gender and ability levels rather than relying solely on voluntary and purposive methods.
The exam-style seating recommendation is practical and directly addresses the risk of participants viewing each otherâs responses, enhancing procedural validity.
The conclusion restates the statistical outcome but does not integrate limitations or proposed modifications into the summary, limiting its evaluative depth.
The reference list relies heavily on primary Loftus & Palmer sources; including more recent replication or meta-analytic studies would strengthen the literature review.
Online sources lack retrieval dates and consistent formatting. Ensure all web references include access dates and adhere consistently to a chosen style guide.
